summary: After finishing my bachelor's degree in zoology with teacher education, I pursued graduate work at Luther Seminary in St. Paul, Minnesota. I spent four years there preparing for ministry, which was a calling I felt strongly about since college. Luther Seminary was where I deepened my theological understanding and equipped myself to serve as a pastor. It was a pivotal step before I moved to Boyceville in 2002 to begin my pastoral career.

I came to Boyceville in August 2002 and have been part of this small town ever since. Boyceville is a tight-knit community in Northwest Wisconsin with just over a thousand people, where everyone tends to know each other’s histories. It’s still largely a farming town, but many residents commute to Menomonie, Eau Claire, or even Minneapolis–Saint Paul for work. As a pastor here, I’m deeply involved in the community, not just through church but also by participating in local activities like announcing sports games and serving as a crossing guard. The town’s strong sense of connection and support has made it a special place to live and serve.

Boyceville is located in Northwest Wisconsin, a region that still holds onto its rural and farming roots. This area includes small towns like Boyceville and nearby Menomonie and Eau Claire, where many people from Boyceville commute for work. Living and working in Northwest Wisconsin has given me a unique perspective on rural community life and the challenges and strengths of these small towns. It’s a region where community ties run deep and people support one another through all phases of life.

Minneapolis–Saint Paul is about an hour and a half west of Boyceville, and it’s a major urban center that many people from my town travel to for work. I attended Luther Seminary in St. Paul, which is part of the Twin Cities, for my graduate studies before moving back to Boyceville. The Twin Cities represent a contrast to the small-town life I now lead, but they have been an important part of my educational and spiritual journey. Despite the distance, the connection between Boyceville and Minneapolis–Saint Paul is strong for many residents who commute or have ties there.
cues: one and a half hours east of Minneapolis–Saint Paul

summary: Menomonie is one of the nearby towns where many people from Boyceville go for work or to access services not available in our small town. It’s about a 15-minute drive from Boyceville and offers more shopping and business opportunities. While Boyceville maintains its small-town charm, Menomonie serves as a hub for the surrounding communities. I often see the connection between Boyceville and Menomonie in the daily lives of the people I serve as pastor.

Eau Claire is another larger town near Boyceville where many residents commute for employment and basic needs. It’s part of the broader region that influences life in Boyceville and provides access to bigger businesses and services. Although I serve in a small town, Eau Claire’s proximity means that people often balance life between these communities. It’s important for me as a pastor to understand these connections because they shape the rhythms of life for my congregation.

The COVID-19 pandemic dramatically changed how I served as a pastor and engaged with the Boyceville community. Starting in 2020, we had to shut down in-person worship and move entirely online, then to outdoor car-based services to keep people safe. It was challenging not being able to visit people in hospitals or nursing homes, and I missed the personal connections that are so vital in a small town. We adapted by livestreaming services, doing drive-through communion, and implementing strict safety protocols like masking and social distancing. Even with restrictions easing, we continue to offer online worship because many still prefer it, and the pandemic taught us new ways to stay connected despite physical distance.

Easter during the pandemic was a unique experience for our church. We provided drive-through communion where I wore a mask and gloves to safely distribute pre-packaged communion elements to people in their cars. This was part of our broader effort to maintain worship and sacramental life despite the challenges of COVID-19. Easter has always been a significant time for our congregation, and even in 2020 and 2021, we found ways to celebrate it meaningfully while keeping everyone safe.
